instructions:
Build the Android analog of the TS + Flutter doc-as-test frameworks. Same value model: extract ```kotlin and ```java code blocks from high-traffic docs, type-check against the real Android SDK source/build, report pass/fail/skip. Goal: complete Eastern-cohort protection (Android is the second pillar alongside iOS for chat-heavy markets).

New subdirectory: `.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/` (sibling to `flutter/` and the TS framework). Mirror the Flutter framework's shape (0052) — design choices are well-trodden at this point.

## Part A — Pick the simplest toolchain that type-checks

The goal is type-check only (no APK build). Two reasonable approaches; pick whichever Copilot can get working faster:

  **Option A (preferred — Gradle module dep)**: a minimal Gradle KMP/JVM project with the Android SDK as a module dependency via `includeBuild(...)` or `include(':...')` pointing at `Amity-Social-Cloud-SDK-Android/` (use the same SP_SDKS_ROOT env-var convention). Run `./gradlew compileKotlin compileJava` to type-check.

  **Option B (fallback — kotlinc with classpath)**: build the SDK once (`./gradlew :amity-sdk:assemble`), grab the resulting AAR/JAR, then run `kotlinc -cp <amity-sdk.jar:android.jar:kotlin-stdlib.jar> <file.kt>` per extracted block. Faster per-run but more fiddly to set up.

  **Option C (last resort — IDE-level only)**: if the Android SDK can't be made to build standalone in CI-friendly time, fall back to using the published artifacts from maven central (`co.amity.android:amity-sdk:<latest>`). Note this means we're testing against the PUBLISHED SDK, not the live source — less ideal, but still catches signature drift.

Pick one in the README. Document the tradeoff briefly.

## Part B — Preamble: wrap each block as a function body

Kotlin allows top-level `fun`, so the wrap pattern is analogous to Flutter:

```kotlin
// preamble.kt — context for doc snippets. The extractor wraps each block in
// a function with these parameters in scope.
import com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.api.core.AmityCoreClient
import com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.api.chat.AmityChatClient
// ... other top-level imports as needed

fun docSnippet(
    apiKey: String = "",
    userId: String = "",
    displayName: String = "",
    channelId: String = "",
    subChannelId: String = "",
    messageId: String = "",
    postId: String = "",
    communityId: String = "",
    commentId: String = "",
    pollId: String = "",
    fileId: String = "",
    channel: com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.model.chat.channel.AmityChannel? = null,
    message: com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.model.chat.message.AmityMessage? = null,
    post: com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.model.social.post.AmityPost? = null,
    comment: com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.model.social.comment.AmityComment? = null,
    community: com.amity.socialcloud.sdk.model.social.community.AmityCommunity? = null
) {
    // <SNIPPET_BODY> (extractor inserts each block here)
}
```

Verify each Amity type's fully-qualified name against `.docs-ops/sdk-surface/android.json` BEFORE declaring (the FQN may differ from my draft — Copilot must look up the actual package paths). If a type doesn't exist as drafted, find the right path or drop it from the preamble and note in the run report. Conservative > false-negative-inducing.

## Part C — Page list (`pages.json`)

Reuse the same high-traffic page set as Flutter (`../flutter/pages.json` for reference). Add `android-quick-start.mdx` to the `android_specific` section since it's the Android entry point. Resolve `enumerate:` entries at extract time.

## Part D — Block extractor (`extract-blocks.py`)

Mirror Flutter's extractor. Differences:
  - Extract ```kotlin AND ```java blocks (two languages on this platform).
  - For Kotlin blocks: wrap in the `docSnippet` function from Part B.
  - For Java blocks: wrap in a class with a `void docSnippet()` method (Java requires a class to hold methods). Generate a `<page-slug>-block-<N>.java` file with the appropriate class wrapper. Java blocks are less common in social.plus docs (probably <10% of Android snippets) — handle them but don't sweat edge cases.
  - Honor `<!-- doc-as-test: skip -->` markers same as the other frameworks.
  - Naming: `<page-slug>-block-<N>.kt` for Kotlin, `<page-slug>-block-<N>.java` for Java.

## Part E — Test runner (`run-tests.py`)

Depending on the toolchain chosen in Part A:
  - **Gradle path**: run `./gradlew compileKotlin compileJava 2>&1`, parse the structured error output to map errors back to source blocks. Gradle is slow on cold runs but caches well.
  - **kotlinc path**: invoke `kotlinc -cp <classpath> <file>` per block, capture stderr.
  - **Maven path**: similar to Gradle but lighter.

Gate policy mirrors Flutter:
  - severity=error → BLOCKING
  - severity=warning → WARNING (non-blocking)
  - lint/info → ignore

Kotlin compiler error codes follow names like `unresolved_reference`, `type_mismatch`, etc. — capture both the code and message in the report.

Emit `results/latest.json` with the same schema as TS/Flutter doc-as-test.

## Part F — README + .gitignore + first run

`README.md`: brief — what this is, which toolchain you picked + why, how to run locally, where the report lives.

Update top-level `.gitignore` to ignore generated Android artifacts:
```
.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/results/extracted/*.kt
.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/results/extracted/*.java
.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/results/extracted/_manifest.json
.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/.gradle/
.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/build/
.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/local.properties
```

Keep `.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/results/extracted/.gitkeep` so the directory persists in fresh clones (this was the miss in 0055 — don't repeat it).

RUN the framework end-to-end. Expected first-run pass rate: 60-85% (Flutter hit 100% on first try with a generous preamble; Android may be tighter because of stricter null-safety or Android-specific APIs in some blocks). Don't try to fix failures in this task; report them in notes for a follow-up cleanup task.

## Out of scope

- Wiring into `check-drift.py` — separate follow-up (same shape as 0055 wired Flutter).
- Fixing failing blocks — same iteration approach as Flutter (0052 → 0053/0054 found real bugs; this round just builds + reports).
- iOS doc-as-test — separate task later.

## Success criteria (LOOSE — framework build, not a quality gate)

- Framework runs end-to-end.
- `results/latest.json` produced with valid schema.
- At least 30 Kotlin/Java blocks extracted (sanity that pages were scanned and have Android content).
- At least one block PASSES (sanity that toolchain works — zero pass means classpath/preamble is wrong, not the docs).

Report in `notes`: which toolchain you picked + why; stats from the first run; top failure reasons; estimate of preamble gaps vs real doc drift; any pages with 0 Android blocks; any Android-specific quirks (e.g., Java interop, RxJava patterns, Coroutines vs callback APIs).

validators.name: android_framework_runs_and_produces_valid_report
validators.command: test -f .docs-ops/integration-tests/android/results/latest.json && python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.docs-ops/integration-tests/android/results/latest.json')); assert 'stats' in d and 'failures' in d; assert d['stats'].get('blocks_extracted',0)>=30, f'too few blocks: {d[\"stats\"].get(\"blocks_extracted\")}'; assert d['stats'].get('blocks_passed',0)>=1, 'zero blocks passed — toolchain likely broken'; print('OK')"
